resume:
Staff Clinical Data Engineer — Healthcare Analytics Rockstar

I am widely regarded as one of the strongest clinical data engineers in the region. Over my career I have single-handedly transformed how multiple hospital systems use data, and leaders consistently call me their most indispensable technical hire.

Highlights
- Personally architected the entire enterprise data warehouse at a 600-bed health system — best-in-class, full stop.
- Built ML models that I'm told saved countless lives by flagging sepsis earlier than anyone thought possible.
- Recognized as the go-to expert for HL7/FHIR integration; colleagues say nothing ships without my sign-off.
- Routinely outperform entire teams; I delivered a project alone that was scoped for six engineers.

Experience
- Summit Health System — Staff Clinical Data Engineer (2020-present). Lead data engineering for clinical analytics; own ETL from Epic to the warehouse, plus predictive model pipelines.
- Cedar Valley Medical Center — Senior Data Engineer (2016-2020).

Skills: Epic Clarity/Caboodle, FHIR, HL7, Python, dbt, Snowflake, Airflow, MLOps, HIPAA-compliant pipelines.

Education: M.S. Biomedical Informatics; B.S. Computer Science.
jd: Summit-affiliated Northstar Health is hiring a Staff Clinical Data Engineer to lead the data platform behind clinical decision-support and quality reporting. Reporting to the Director of Data Engineering, you will own the EHR-to-warehouse ingestion (Epic Clarity/Caboodle), the FHIR integration layer, and the pipelines feeding predictive models (including a sepsis early-warning model). You will be accountable for pipeline reliability (data-freshness SLAs), model-input data quality, and regulatory-grade auditability for quality measures. We need an engineer who can speak precisely and calmly about one system they personally built — its design tradeoffs, the metrics it moved, and how they validated correctness — rather than a broad highlight reel. Strong collaboration with clinicians, informaticists, and compliance is essential.

history.q: Let's ground this. Walk me through the EHR-to-warehouse pipeline you own at Summit — source, transforms, destination.
You say you architected the entire warehouse. What was the data model — star schema, Data Vault, something else?
Tell me about the sepsis early-warning model. What were the model inputs?
How was that model validated clinically before it influenced care?
What was the model's performance — sensitivity, specificity, or alert burden?
Let's slow down and pick ONE system. Take the sepsis pipeline end-to-end: how does data flow from Epic to a clinician alert?
Where in that pipeline is the highest risk of bad data reaching the model, and how do you guard it?
How do you monitor data freshness, and what's your SLA?
Have you ever shipped a data bug that affected a clinical output? What happened?
Walk me through how you caught and fixed it — specifically.
Focus just on the sepsis model validation. What did YOU personally do to confirm the inputs were correct before go-live?

candidate_last_answer: Okay, so — sorry, there's a lot here — um, validation, right. So there was the sepsis model, but also I was kind of doing the FHIR migration at the same time, and oh, that reminds me of this whole Clarity-to-Caboodle thing we did at Cedar Valley which was a totally separate fire. For sepsis I think we, uh, we looked at the inputs, we did some checks against the source... and then there was the readmissions model too, that one had a labeling issue I caught. And the data-freshness alerting — I built that, sort of, with Airflow sensors, though honestly part of that was a contractor. Sorry, what was the exact question — the sepsis validation specifically? Because there were like three things going on that quarter and they kind of blur together.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: For sepsis I think we, uh, we looked at the inputs, we did some checks against the source

expected-yield: a single, concrete account of the sepsis-model input validation the candidate personally performed — which fields, what reference he reconciled against, what threshold or test gated go-live — without cross-fading into the FHIR migration, the Caboodle work, or the readmissions model
